1: bayyôm haššəlîšî — "on the third day." The three-day delay during which Ziklag was burned matches the travel time from Aphek. Amalêq (Amalek) — the same enemy Saul failed to destroy in 1 Samuel 15. The destruction visited on David's base is precisely the enemy that Saul's disobedience left alive. The theological link between 1 Samuel 15:7-9 and 30:1 is structural: Saul's incomplete ḥērem now strikes at YHWH's chosen king.
2: wəlōʾ hemîtû ʾîš — "killing no one." The Amalekites' restraint in not killing the captives makes total recovery possible; the verb hemîtû (Hiphil of mût, to kill) marks the exception. The captive-taking rather than slaughter anticipates the total recovery — nothing is permanently lost.
3: The discovery sequence: they came to the city, found it burned (śərûpāh ʾēš), wives and children gone. The verb wəhinnēh (and behold/look) introduces the shocking sight — a narrative particle that places the reader alongside the arriving soldiers at the moment of discovery.
4: wayyiśśəʾû ʾet qôlām wayyiḇkû ʿad ʾăšer lōʾ hāyāh lāhem kōaḥ liḇkôt — "they wept aloud until they had no more strength to weep." The physical exhaustion of grief — weeping until strength is gone — is measured precisely. Kōaḥ (H3581, strength/power) spent on mourning; it will need to be restored (v6).
5: The specific naming of David's two wives — Ahinoam and Abigail — personalizes the crisis: this is not only his men's tragedy but his own. Šəḇuyôt (captives, from šāḇāh, H7617, to take captive) — the same root as the broader captivity of Israel in later periods; the individual event uses the vocabulary of national catastrophe.
6: wayyiṯḥazzēq dāwid baYHWH ʾĕlōhāyw — "David strengthened himself in the LORD his God." The Hithpael of ḥāzaq (H2388) is uniquely reflexive: David is the agent of his own strengthening, drawing from YHWH the strength he needs. This is active faith — not passive reception but deliberate turning toward YHWH in the moment of crisis. The contrast with Saul (1 Samuel 28:5: wayyirāʾ, was terrified; 1 Samuel 28:7: seeking a medium) is the chapter's theological hinge. ʾĕlōhāyw — "his God" — the possessive marks covenantal relationship; YHWH is David's specifically, not only Israel's generically.
7: haggîšāh nāʾ lî ʾet hāʾēpôd — "bring me the ephod." David immediately turns to the legitimate means of inquiry — the priestly ephod — rather than any illicit channel. Abiathar (son of Ahimelech, the priest Saul had murdered at Nob, 1 Samuel 22) is with David; the escaped priest enables the inquiry Saul could not access. The contrast with 1 Samuel 28:6 (Urim closed to Saul) is pointed: the same sacred apparatus answers David but was silent to Saul.
8: nāśōg tāśîg wəhaṣṣēl taṣṣîl — "you will certainly overtake them and certainly rescue the captives." Two infinitive absolute + imperfect constructions in sequence — the doubled certainty idiom in Hebrew. Nāśag (H5381, to overtake/reach) + hiṣṣîl (Hiphil of nāṣal, H5337, to deliver/rescue). The double absolute construction removes all uncertainty: YHWH's promise is unconditional and total. Compare 15:3 where the ḥērem command was also absolute — what Saul violated, David will fulfill in reverse through complete recovery.
9: naḥal habbesôr — the brook Besor. The name is uncertain but may derive from a root meaning "fresh water." The brook divides those who go on (400) from those who remain (200). This geographical dividing point becomes the occasion for David's ruling in v24.
10: wayirdōp dāwid hûʾ wəʾarbaʿ mēʾôt ʾîš — David goes with 400; 200 "were too exhausted (pāgar, H6296, to be faint/worn out) to cross the Besor." Pāgar denotes physical depletion — they were not cowardly but depleted. The distinction matters for David's ruling in v24.
11: The Egyptian slave found in the field functions as a providential discovery — an abandoned source of intelligence. Wayyimṣəʾû ʾîš miṣrî baśśādeh — "they found an Egyptian in the field" — the passive discovery (māṣāʾ, to find) rather than active pursuit. Food and water are immediately given before any questioning — basic hospitality before information extraction.
12: wayyittənû lô peleaḥ dəḇēlāh ûšətê ṣimmûqîm — fig cake and raisin clusters — concentrated energy food for a man three days without sustenance. Wattāšob rûḥô ʾēlāyw — "his strength/spirit returned to him" (rûaḥ, H7307, spirit/breath). The restorative function of food and drink prepares the Egyptian to serve as guide.
13: The Egyptian's story: slave of an Amalekite, abandoned when ill three days ago. Wayyaʿazbēnî ʾădōnî kî ḥālîtî — "my master abandoned me when I fell sick." The contrast with David's treatment of his men (vv9-10) is implicit: David does not abandon the exhausted at Besor; the Amalekite abandoned a sick slave. The abandoned slave leads David to the raiders.
14: The Egyptian's geographical inventory of the raid: Negeb of the Cherethites, Negeb of Judah, Negeb of Caleb, then Ziklag burned. Nəḡeḇ (H5045, south/Negeb region) — the semi-arid zone south of Judah where David operated. The Cherethites are associated with the Philistine sphere; the raid covered both Philistine and Judahite territory, explaining why both David (Philistine subject) and Judah (Israelite territory) were hit.
15: wəhišbaʿ lî bēʾlōhîm ʾim timitēnî — "swear to me by God that you won't kill me or hand me back to my master." The oath invokes ʾĕlōhîm — the Egyptian uses the generic divine name rather than YHWH; David swears by the God of Israel to an Egyptian. The exchange is entirely practical and mutually beneficial — no coercion on David's side.
16: pəšûṭîm ʿal pənê kol hāʾāreṣ ʾōḵəlîm wəšōṯîm wəḥōgĕgîm — "spread out across the land, eating, drinking, and celebrating." The Amalekites are caught in post-victory celebration, which renders them militarily vulnerable. Ḥōgĕgîm (from ḥāgag, H2287, to celebrate a feast/dance) — pilgrimage-festival language applied to pagan revelry. The verb's sacred connotation in Israel makes its use here ironic.
17: wayyakkēm dāwid minnešep wəʿad hāʿereb limmāḥărātām — "David struck them from twilight until the evening of the following day." The extended battle time — overnight into the next day — signals determined, total pursuit. Lōʾ nimlaṭ mēhem ʾîš kî ʾim ʾarbaʿ mēʾôt ʾîš naʿar — only 400 young men escaped on camels. The completeness of the defeat (with only the camel-mounted escape force surviving) contrasts with Saul's incomplete destruction of Amalek in 1 Samuel 15.
18: wayyaṣṣēl dāwid ʾēt kol ʾăšer lāqəḥû hāʾămālēqîm — "David recovered everything the Amalekites had taken." The verb yaṣṣēl (Hiphil of nāṣal, same root as the promise in v8) — the promise and its fulfillment use identical vocabulary. Wəʾēt šətê nāšāyw hiṣṣîl dāwid — "including his two wives." The personal element of recovery is specified first before the general summary in v19.
19: wəlōʾ neʾdar lāhem minnaqṭōn wəʿad gādôl ûminbānîm ûḇānôt ûmišlāl — "nothing was missing — young or old, sons or daughters, any plunder." The Hebrew uses neʾdar (Niphal of ʿādar, H5737, to be missing/lacking) — the negative of "lacking." Total recovery is expressed as the absence of any deficit. The comprehensive list reinforces the double absolute certainty of v8: YHWH's word proved exactly true.
20: wayyiqḥ dāwid ʾet kol haṣṣōʾn wəhabbāqār — David also seizes the livestock beyond what was originally taken from Ziklag. The men acknowledge this as David's spoil (šəlal dāwid zeh) — he has gained more than he lost. The surplus of recovery over loss will govern the distribution dispute of vv22-25.
21: wayyēlek dāwid ʾel māʾtāyim hāʾănāšîm ʾăšer pāgərû — "David returned to the two hundred men who had been too exhausted to follow." Wayyišʾal lāhem ləšālôm — "he greeted them warmly" (literally "asked them of peace"). The formal greeting of peace (šālôm) before any question of spoil-sharing is deliberate — David establishes relational regard before the economic dispute surfaces.
22: ʾanšê hārāʿ wəhaббəlîyāʿal — "wicked and worthless men" (rāʿ, H7451, evil; bəlîyāʿal, H1100, worthlessness — literally "without profit/value"). Bəlîyāʿal is a term of contempt in the DtrH corpus (1 Samuel 2:12, Eli's sons; Judges 19:22; 1 Samuel 20:13). Their ruling — "they get only their wives and children" — reduces the exhausted men to non-participants. The justice issue is whether combat service alone qualifies for equal share.
23: lōʾ tǝʿăśû kēn ʾaḥay ʾēt ʾăšer nātan YHWH lānû — "No, my brothers — that is not right with what the LORD has given us." David's counter grounds the ruling in theology, not equity: the spoil is YHWH's gift (nātan), not the warriors' earnings. Wayyišmor ʾōtānû — "he kept us safe" — YHWH's protection of those who fought is the source of success, making human effort the secondary cause.
24: kəḥēleq hayyōrēd bamməlḥāmāh ûkəḥēleq hayyōšēḇ ʿal hakkēlîm yəḥaddāw yəḥallēqû — "the share of the one who goes into battle is the same as the share of the one who stays with the supplies — they share equally." Kēlîm (H3627, vessels/equipment/supplies) — the "baggage" is military equipment, not personal belongings. The equal-share ruling is the practical expression of the theological principle: victory is YHWH's, the spoil is his gift, and the distribution reflects covenantal solidarity rather than proportional performance.
25: wayhî mēhayyôm hahûʾ wāmāʿlāh wayyəśîmēhā ləḥōq ûlemiṣpāṭ — "from that day onward David made it a fixed rule (ḥōq, H2706) and ordinance (miṣpāṭ, H4941) for Israel." Ḥōq + miṣpāṭ is the paired legal terminology of covenant law (cf. Exodus 15:25; Deut passim). David legislates at the moment of crisis — the ad hoc ruling becomes constitutional precedent.
26: wayyišlaḥ miššəlāl lə ziqnê yəhûdāh lərēʿēhû — "he sent portions of the spoil to the elders of Judah — his friends." Rēʿa (H7453, neighbor/friend) — the same word used in 15:28 and 28:17 for David as Saul's "neighbor" who received the kingdom. Now David proactively builds covenant relationships through gift-giving. The list that follows (vv27-31) is a geographical network of Judahite loyalty-building.
27: The distribution towns — Bethel, Ramoth-negeb, Jattir — are in the Negeb of Judah, the region David traversed during his outlaw period. The gifts retroactively honor communities that provided cover and resources during David's years of flight.
28: Aroer, Siphmoth, Eshtemoa — further Judahite communities in the Negeb. The distribution is comprehensive rather than strategic — every community that sheltered David receives acknowledgment.
29: Racal, the Jerahmeelite towns, the Kenite towns — extending the gift-network to non-Israelite groups in the Negeb who maintained relationship with David. The Kenites (earlier spared from the Amalekite campaign at 1 Samuel 15:6) reappear as allies.
30: Hormah, Bor-ashan, Athach — locations in the Negeb district; several unidentified with certainty. The list's completeness is the point: no community is overlooked, establishing David as a king who remembers those who helped him.
31: ûbəḥeḇrôn ûḇəkol hammaqōmôt ʾăšer hithallak šām dāwid hûʾ waʾănāšāyw — "in Hebron — everywhere David and his men had wandered." Heḇrôn (Hebron) appears last and climactically: it will be the site of David's first anointing as king (2 Samuel 2:1-4). The gift-network of 1 Samuel 30 anticipates the political base that will enable the transition of 2 Samuel.
